---
title: "03_sequencer_comparison"
output: html_document
---

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(ggtext)
library(patchwork)
library(gt)
library(writexl)
```

```{r}
# ==============================================================================
# CONFIGURATION: ANALYSIS SETTINGS
# ==============================================================================

# 1. Number of Top Abundant Taxa to Highlight in Figures
#    This setting controls how many of the most abundant taxa are colored in
#    the scatter plots (Figure 2, Supp Fig S1) and MA plots (Supp Fig S2).
#    All other taxa will be shown in gray.
top_n_to_highlight <- 15

# ==============================================================================
```

```{r}
# --- 3.1 Analyze Quantitative Correlation (Final Consolidated Version) ---

# Create a directory for figures if it doesn't exist
if (!dir.exists("figures")) {
  dir.create("figures")
}

# --- 3.1.1 Data Preparation ---

# Load libraries and the processed data from the previous Rmd file
# (This part would typically be at the top of your Rmd script)
illumina_processed <- readRDS(file = "processed_data/illumina_processed.rds")
nanopore_processed <- readRDS(file = "processed_data/nanopore_processed.rds")

# --- 3.1.2 Data Transformation for Readability ---
# Create a "Genus species" format in the species column for clearer interpretation,
# with a special exception for TM7-related taxa in the ONT data.

# --- For Illumina data ---
illumina_processed <- illumina_processed %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    # Only combine if both genus and species are available (not NA)
    species = dplyr::if_else(
      !is.na(genus) & !is.na(species),
      stringr::str_c(genus, species, sep = " "), # Combine them with a space
      species # Otherwise, keep the original species value
    )
  )

# --- For ONT data (with TM7 exception) ---
nanopore_processed <- nanopore_processed %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    species = dplyr::if_else(
      # Condition to combine:
      # 1. Both genus and species are available (not NA)
      # 2. AND the species column does NOT contain the string "TM7" or starts with "Canditatus"
      !is.na(genus) & !is.na(species) & !stringr::str_detect(species, "TM7") & !str_starts(species, "Candidatus"),
      
      # If TRUE, combine them with a space
      stringr::str_c(genus, species, sep = " "),
      
      # If FALSE (if it's TM7 or if species/genus is NA), keep the original value
      species 
    )
  )

# --- Optional but Recommended: Filtering Step ---
abundance_threshold <- 0.1
#abundance_threshold <- 0.01
#abundance_threshold <- 0

illumina_filtered <- illumina_processed %>%
  dplyr::filter(if_any(where(is.numeric), ~ .x >= abundance_threshold))

nanopore_filtered <- nanopore_processed %>%
  dplyr::filter(if_any(where(is.numeric), ~ .x >= abundance_threshold))

# --- Convert both datasets to a "long" format ---
illumina_long <- illumina_filtered %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(AP, BP, AS, BS),
    names_to = "Sample",
    values_to = "Abundance"
  )

nanopore_long <- nanopore_filtered %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(AP, BP, AS, BS),
    names_to = "Sample",
    values_to = "Abundance"
  )

# --- 3.1.3 Loop Through Taxonomic Levels for Analysis ---

# Define the taxonomic levels to analyze
tax_levels_to_analyze <- c("phylum", "family", "genus", "species")

# Create an empty tibble to store the correlation summary statistics
correlation_summary_table <- tibble()

# Start a loop
for (tax_level in tax_levels_to_analyze) {
  
  # --- Aggregate and Merge Data ---
  illumina_agg <- illumina_long %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Sample, !!rlang::sym(tax_level)) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(Abundance = sum(Abundance), .groups = 'drop') %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(!!rlang::sym(tax_level)))

  nanopore_agg <- nanopore_long %>%
    dplyr::group_by(Sample, !!rlang::sym(tax_level)) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(Abundance = sum(Abundance), .groups = 'drop') %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(!!rlang::sym(tax_level)))
    
  merged_data <- dplyr::full_join(
    illumina_agg,
    nanopore_agg,
    by = c(tax_level, "Sample"),
    suffix = c("_illumina", "_nanopore")
  ) %>%
    replace(is.na(.), 0) %>% 
    dplyr::arrange(!!rlang::sym(tax_level), Sample) 
    
  
  # --- Create a dynamic object for the merged data in the environment ---
  object_name <- paste0("merged_data_", tax_level)
  assign(object_name, merged_data)

  # --- Calculate Overall Spearman's Correlation ---
  cor_test <- cor.test(
    merged_data$Abundance_illumina,
    merged_data$Abundance_nanopore,
    method = "spearman"
  )
  
  # --- Add the results to the summary table ---
  current_level_summary <- tibble(
    Taxonomic_Level = tax_level,
    Spearman_rho = cor_test$estimate,
    p_value = cor_test$p.value
  )
  correlation_summary_table <- dplyr::bind_rows(correlation_summary_table, current_level_summary)


 # --- Create Scatter Plot with Top Taxa Highlighted ---

  # 1. Identify the top 10 most abundant taxa
  top_taxa <- merged_data %>%
    dplyr::group_by(!!rlang::sym(tax_level)) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(mean_abundance = mean(Abundance_illumina + Abundance_nanopore)) %>%
    dplyr::slice_max(order_by = mean_abundance, n = top_n_to_highlight) %>%
    dplyr::pull(!!rlang::sym(tax_level))
  
  # 2. Prepare the data for plotting
  merged_data_for_plot <- merged_data %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      Taxon_Color = dplyr::if_else(
        # Check against 'top_taxa' list
        !!rlang::sym(tax_level) %in% top_taxa,
        !!rlang::sym(tax_level),
        "Other"
      ),
      # Create the factor using the 'top_taxa' list
      Taxon_Color = factor(Taxon_Color, levels = c(top_taxa, "Other"))
    )
  
  # 3. Create a named color palette (Using Tableau 20 colors for better visibility)
  # This replaces RColorBrewer to avoid visibility issues and ensure distinct colors
  tableau_20_colors <- c(
    "#1f77b4", "#ff7f0e", "#2ca02c", "#d62728", "#9467bd", 
    "#8c564b", "#e377c2", "#7f7f7f", "#bcbd22", "#17becf", 
    "#aec7e8", "#ffbb78", "#98df8a", "#ff9896", "#c5b0d5", 
    "#c49c94", "#f7b6d2", "#c7c7c7", "#dbdb8d", "#9edae5"
  )
  
  num_highlight_taxa <- length(top_taxa)
  
  # Select the needed number of colors from the custom palette
  # If we need more than 20 (unlikely), we recycle or use ramp
  if (num_highlight_taxa <= 20) {
    highlight_colors <- tableau_20_colors[1:num_highlight_taxa]
  } else {
     highlight_colors <- colorRampPalette(tableau_20_colors)(num_highlight_taxa)
  }
  
  # Create a named vector
  color_values <- setNames(highlight_colors, top_taxa)
  # Add the color for "Other"
  color_values["Other"] <- "grey70"

  # 4. Create Markdown-enabled labels for the legend
  # Create a named vector where names are the original taxon names
  # and values are the formatted labels (with italics)
  markdown_labels <- setNames(
    paste0("*", top_taxa, "*"),
    top_taxa
  )
  # Add the "Other" label, which has no asterisks
  markdown_labels["Other"] <- "Other" 
  
  # 5. Create the plot
  
  ## Define a small constant for the pseudocount
  ## pseudocount <- 0.001
  
  # --- Create the Scatter Plot (with pseudocount for handling zeros) ---
  correlation_plot <- ggplot(
    merged_data_for_plot, 
    #aes(x = Abundance_illumina + pseudocount,
    #    y = Abundance_nanopore + pseudocount)
    #) +
    aes(x = Abundance_illumina, y = Abundance_nanopore)
    )+ 
      
    # Add a line for 10-fold difference (Nanopore > Illumina)
    # The intercept is log10(10), which is 1
    geom_abline(intercept = 1, slope = 1, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
    
    # Add a line for 10-fold difference (Illumina > Nanopore)
    # The intercept is log10(0.1), which is -1
    geom_abline(intercept = -1, slope = 1, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
    
    # Add the y=x identity line (now in black)
    geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, color = "black", linetype = "dashed") +
    
    #geom_point(aes(color = Taxon_Color), alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
    geom_point(
      aes(
        color = Taxon_Color,
        size = (Abundance_illumina + Abundance_nanopore) / 2, 
        ),
      alpha = 0.7
      ) +
   
    scale_x_continuous(trans = 'log10', labels = scales::label_number()) +
    scale_y_continuous(trans = 'log10', labels = scales::label_number()) +
    
    # Use the new, correctly named color vector
    scale_color_manual(
      name = stringr::str_to_title(tax_level),
      values = color_values,
      # Use markdown labels for the legend text
      labels = markdown_labels
    ) +
    
    labs(
      x = "Illumina abundance (%)",
      y = "ONT abundance (%)",
      size = "Average abundance (%)"
    ) +
    theme_bw(base_size = 14) +
    theme(
      legend.title = element_text(size = 12),
      legend.text = ggtext::element_markdown(size = 10), # small text size
      legend.key.height = unit(0.45, "cm"), # tight vertical spacing
      legend.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0) # remove extra margin
          )+
    annotate("text", 
             x = min(merged_data_for_plot$Abundance_illumina[merged_data_for_plot$Abundance_illumina > 0]), 
             #x = pseudocount,
             y = max(merged_data_for_plot$Abundance_nanopore), 
             #label = paste("rho ==", round(cor_test$estimate, 3)), 
             #parse = TRUE, hjust = 0, vjust = 1, size = 5) +
             label = paste("ρ =", round(cor_test$estimate, 3)), 
             hjust = 0, vjust = 1, size = 5) +
    
    # To make the horizontal legend fit, you can arrange it in multiple rows
    guides(
      # Control the COLOR legend:
      # Keep 1 column (ncol = 1) but pack items tightly
      color = guide_legend(
        ncol = 1,
        title.position = "top",
        order = 1),
      
      # Control the SIZE legend: arrange items horizontally
      # size = guide_legend(nrow = 1, direction = "horizontal", title.position = "top", order = 2)
      size = "none"
    )

  # Dynamically assign the created plot to a unique object name
  # e.g., correlation_plot_phylum, correlation_plot_genus
  plot_object_name <- paste0("correlation_plot_", tax_level)
  assign(plot_object_name, correlation_plot)
  
  # --- Save the plot to a file using ggsave() ---
  ggsave(
    # Create a dynamic filename, e.g., "figures/correlation_plot_genus.png"
    filename = paste0("figures/correlation_plot_", tax_level, ".png"),
    plot = correlation_plot,
    width = 8,
    height = 6,
    dpi = 300
  )
  
  # --- Print Results ---
  print(paste("--- Correlation Plot for:", tax_level, "---"))
  print(correlation_plot)
  print(paste("--> Created object:", object_name))
  print("-----------------------------------------")
}

# --- Conbine the merged data to an .xlsx file for Supplementary Table S1 --
# Combine the four data frames into a single list
# The list names (e.g., "phylum") will become the Excel sheet names
s1_data_list <- list(
  "phylum" = merged_data_phylum,
  "family" = merged_data_family,
  "genus" = merged_data_genus,
  "species" = merged_data_species
)
# Write the list to a single .xlsx file
write_xlsx(s1_data_list, "tables/Supplementary_Table_S1.xlsx")

# --- 3.1.4 Combine Phylum and Genus Plots into Figure 2 ---

# Combine the two specific plots we want for Figure 2
figure_2 <- correlation_plot_phylum + correlation_plot_genus +
  plot_layout(axis_titles = 'collect_y') +
  # Collect legends from both plots into a single, shared legend block
  #plot_layout(guides = 'collect') +
  # Automatically add "A" and "B" labels to the top-left of each panel
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'A') & # Use '&' to apply theme to subplots
  # Fine-tune the appearance of the A/B tags
  theme(
    #plot.tag.position = "bottom",
    plot.tag = element_text(size = 18, face = "plain")
    )

ggsave(
  filename = "figures/fig2_correlation_comparison.png",
  plot = figure_2,
  width = 12,
  height = 4,
  dpi = 300
)

# Finally, display the combined figure in your R Markdown output
print(figure_2)


# --- 3.1.5 Create Supplementary Figure S1 (Family and Species Correlation) ---
# Combine the two plots horizontally (A) Family | (B) Species
supplementary_figure_s1 <- correlation_plot_family + correlation_plot_species +
  plot_layout(axis_titles = "collect_y") +
  # Automatically add "(A)" and "(B)" labels to the panels
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'A') & 
  
  # Apply theme settings to both plots (using '&')
  theme(
    # Style the A/B tags
    plot.tag = element_text(size = 18, face = "plain")
  )

# Save the combined plot to the 'figures' directory
ggsave(
  filename = "figures/Supplementary_Figure_S1.png",
  plot = supplementary_figure_s1,
  width = 12,
  height = 4, 
  dpi = 300
)

# Print the figure to the RMarkdown output
print(supplementary_figure_s1)


# --- 3.1.6 Final Correlation Summary Table ---
# After the loop, print the final summary table
print("--- Overall Correlation Summary Table ---")
print(correlation_summary_table)

# You can now also access the merged data for each level directly
# head(merged_data_genus)
```


```{r}
# --- 3.1.4 Display Final Correlation Summary as a Presentation Table ---

# Make sure the 'gt' package is installed and loaded
# install.packages("gt")
library(gt)

# Manually format the p-value before creating the table ---
correlation_summary_table_formatted <- correlation_summary_table %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    # Create a new column with formatted p-values
    p_value_formatted = dplyr::case_when(
      p_value < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001",
      .default = as.character(round(p_value, 3)) # Otherwise, round to 3 decimals
    )
  )

# Take the summary table we created and format it with the gt package
correlation_table_gt <- correlation_summary_table_formatted %>%
  # Start the gt table object
  gt() %>%
  
  # Hide the original, unformatted p-value column
  cols_hide(columns = p_value) %>%

    # Add a main title and subtitle
  tab_header(
    title = "Table 2 Overall correlation between sequencing platforms",
  ) %>%
  
  # Format the numeric columns for better readability
  fmt_number(
    columns = Spearman_rho,
    decimals = 2 # Round rho to 2 decimal places
  ) %>%
  
  # Relabel the columns to be more descriptive
  cols_label(
    Taxonomic_Level = "Taxonomic Level",
    Spearman_rho = html("Spearman's &rho;"), # Use html() for Greek letter rho
    p_value_formatted = html("<i>p</i>-value")
  ) %>%
  
  # Center-align all columns
  cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = everything()
  ) %>%
  
  # Apply modern table options
  tab_options(
    # Make column labels bold
    column_labels.font.weight = "bold",
    # Remove all horizontal lines in the table body
    table_body.hlines.style = "none",
    # Keep only the line under the column labels
    column_labels.border.bottom.style = "solid",
    column_labels.border.bottom.width = px(2),
    # Remove the top and bottom borders of the whole table
    table.border.top.style = "none",
    table.border.bottom.style = "none"
  ) %>%
  
  # Add a source note at the bottom
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Correlation was calculated across all data points from 4 samples."
  )

# It's good practice to create a folder for your tables
if (!dir.exists("tables")) {
  dir.create("tables")
}

# Save as a PNG image file 
gtsave(
  data = correlation_table_gt,
  filename = "tables/Table2_correlation_summary.png"
)

# Save as an RTF file 
gtsave(
  data = correlation_table_gt,
  filename = "tables/Table2_correlation_summary.rtf"
)

# Finally, display the table in your R Markdown output
correlation_table_gt

```

```{r}
# --- 3.2 Per-Taxon Correlation and Bias Analysis ---

# --- 3.2.1 Data Preparation ---
# We will start with the long-format, filtered data from the previous steps
# (illumina_long, nanopore_long)

# Merge the two long datasets into a single, wide-format table.
# This table will have one row per taxon per sample.
merged_long_data <- dplyr::full_join(
  illumina_long,
  nanopore_long,
  by = c("kingdom", "phylum", "class", "order", "family", "genus", "species", "Sample"),
  suffix = c("_illumina", "_nanopore")
) %>%
  # IMPORTANT FIX: Replace NAs with 0 only in the numeric abundance columns
  # This prevents the "Can't convert <double> to <character>" error.
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(
    .cols = c(Abundance_illumina, Abundance_nanopore),
    .fns = ~ tidyr::replace_na(., 0)
  ))


# --- 3.2.2 Perform Analysis for Phylum and Genus levels ---

# Define the levels to analyze
tax_levels_to_analyze <- c("phylum", "family", "genus", "species")

for (tax_level in tax_levels_to_analyze) {
  
  # First, aggregate the data at the current level (per sample)
  agg_data <- merged_long_data %>%
    dplyr::group_by(!!rlang::sym(tax_level), Sample) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      Abundance_illumina = sum(Abundance_illumina),
      Abundance_nanopore = sum(Abundance_nanopore),
      .groups = 'drop'
    ) %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(!!rlang::sym(tax_level)))

  # Now, group by taxon and calculate statistics across the 4 samples
  summary_table <- agg_data %>%
    dplyr::group_by(!!rlang::sym(tax_level)) %>%
    # A correlation can only be calculated if there are at least 3 pairs of non-zero data points.
    # This filter helps prevent errors with very sparse taxa.
    dplyr::filter(n() >= 3) %>% 
    dplyr::summarise(
      # Spearman's correlation between the 4 illumina values and 4 nanopore values
      correlation = cor(Abundance_illumina, Abundance_nanopore, method = "spearman"),
      
      # Mean abundance for each platform
      mean_illumina = mean(Abundance_illumina),
      sd_illumina = sd(Abundance_illumina),
      mean_nanopore = mean(Abundance_nanopore),
      sd_nanopore = sd(Abundance_nanopore),
      
      .groups = 'drop'
    ) %>%
  
    # Calculate Log2 Fold Change instead of a simple ratio
  dplyr::mutate(
    # Add a small pseudocount (0.001) to avoid log(0) or division by zero
    log2_fold_change = log2((mean_nanopore + 0.001) / (mean_illumina + 0.001))
  ) %>%
  # Sort by the absolute value of the log2 fold change to find the biggest differences
  dplyr::arrange(desc(abs(log2_fold_change)))
  
  # --- Create a dynamic object name and assign the summary table ---
  object_name <- paste0("per_taxon_summary_", tax_level)
  assign(object_name, summary_table)
  
  # --- Print the results for the current level ---
  print(paste("--- Per-Taxon Summary Table for:", tax_level, "---"))
  print(head(get(object_name))) # Print the first few rows of the created table
  print(paste("--> Created object:", object_name))
  print("-----------------------------------------")
}

# --- 3.2.3 Accessing the Created Objects ---
# After the loop, you will see the new objects in your RStudio Environment pane.
# You can now access each one directly by its full name.

# Example:
# head(per_taxon_summary_genus)
```
```{r}
# --- 3.3 Visualizing Bias with MA Plots ---

# Define the levels to analyze
tax_levels_to_analyze <- c("phylum", "family", "genus", "species")

for (tax_level in tax_levels_to_analyze) {
  
  # Get the appropriate summary table from the previous step (2.5)
  summary_table <- get(paste0("per_taxon_summary_", tax_level))

  # --- Prepare data for MA plot ---
  ma_plot_data <- summary_table %>%
    # Calculate the overall average abundance across both platforms
    mutate(average_abundance = (mean_illumina + mean_nanopore) / 2)

  # --- Identify Top 15 and create color column ---
  # Identify the top 15 most ABUNDANT taxa
  top_taxa <- ma_plot_data %>%
    slice_max(order_by = average_abundance, n = top_n_to_highlight) %>%
    pull(!!rlang::sym(tax_level))
  
  # Add a new column for coloring
  ma_plot_data_for_plot <- ma_plot_data %>%
    mutate(
      Taxon_Color = if_else(
        !!rlang::sym(tax_level) %in% top_taxa,
        !!rlang::sym(tax_level),
        "Other"
      ),
      # Convert to a factor to control the order in the legend
      Taxon_Color = factor(Taxon_Color, levels = c(top_taxa, "Other"))
    )

  # --- Create the named color palette ---
  num_top_taxa <- length(top_taxa)
  
  # Handle cases where num_top_taxa might be > 20
    if(num_top_taxa <= 20) {
     top_colors <- tableau_20_colors[1:num_top_taxa]
  } else {
     top_colors <- colorRampPalette(tableau_20_colors)(num_top_taxa)
  }
  
  #top_colors <- RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(max(3, num_top_taxa), "Paired")[1:num_top_taxa]
  color_values <- setNames(top_colors, top_taxa)
  color_values["Other"] <- "grey70"
  
  # Create Markdown-enabled labels for the legend
  # Create a named vector where names are the original taxon names
  # and values are the formatted labels (with italics)
  markdown_labels <- setNames(
    paste0("*", top_taxa, "*"), # Wrap taxon names in asterisks
    top_taxa
  )
  # Add the "Other" label, which has no asterisks
  markdown_labels["Other"] <- "Other"
  
  
  # --- Create the MA Plot ---
  ma_plot_obj <- ggplot(ma_plot_data_for_plot, aes(x = average_abundance, y = log2_fold_change)) +
    geom_point(aes(color = Taxon_Color), size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
    scale_x_log10(labels = scales::label_number()) +
    
    # Use the correctly named color vector
    scale_color_manual(
      #name = paste("Top", length(top_taxa) , stringr::str_to_title(tax_level)),
      name = stringr::str_to_title(tax_level),
      values = color_values, 
      labels= markdown_labels
    ) +
    
    labs(
      x = "Average Abundance (%) [log10 scale]",
      y = "Log2 Fold Change (ONT / Illumina)"
    ) +
    theme_bw(base_size = 14) +
    theme(
      legend.title = element_text(size = 12),
      legend.text = ggtext::element_markdown(size = 10), # small text size
      #legend.key.height = unit(0.45, "cm"), # tight vertical spacing
      legend.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0) # remove extra margin
          )

  # --- Dynamically assign the created plot to a unique object name ---
  # e.g., ma_plot_phylum, ma_plot_genus
  plot_object_name <- paste0("ma_plot_", tax_level)
  assign(plot_object_name, ma_plot_obj)
  
  print(paste("--> Created object:", plot_object_name))
  print(ma_plot_obj)
}
```

```{r}
# --- 3.3.1 Combine MA Plots into Supplementary Figure S2 ---

# Combine the four plots using patchwork in a 2x2 grid
supplementary_figure_s2 <- ma_plot_phylum + ma_plot_family + ma_plot_genus + ma_plot_species +
  # Specify a 2-column layout
  plot_layout(
    ncol = 2,
    axis_titles = "keep",
    # Keep guides (legends) separate as you wanted
    guides = "keep" 
  ) +
  # Add A, B, C, D tags
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'A') &
  
  # Apply theme settings to all plots (using '&')
  theme(
    # Style the A/B/C/D tags
    plot.tag = element_text(size = 18, face = "plain")
  )

# Save the combined plot
ggsave(
  filename = "figures/Supplementary_Figure_S2.png",
  plot = supplementary_figure_s2,
  width = 12, # 2 columns
  height = 10, # 2 rows
  dpi = 300
)

# Print the figure to the RMarkdown output
print(supplementary_figure_s2)
```


```{r}
# --- 3.4 Create Hierarchical Summary Table of Biases ---

# Make sure the 'gt' package is loaded
library(gt)

# --- 1. Prepare data for the hierarchical table ---

# Create a map to know which genus belongs to which phylum
genus_phylum_map <- illumina_processed %>%
  dplyr::select(phylum, genus) %>%
  dplyr::distinct() %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(genus) & !is.na(phylum))

# List the key taxa to highlight
phyla_of_interest <- c("Bacteroidota", "Pseudomonadota", "Actinomycetota", "Bacillota", "Patescibacteria", "Campylobacterota", "Fusobacteriota")
genera_of_interest <- c("Veillonella", "Haemophilus", "Campylobacter", "Actinomyces", "Porphyromonas", "Lautropia", "Prevotella", "Rothia", "Selenomonas", "Nanosynbacter", "Corynebacterium", "Pseudoleptotrichia", "Fusobacterium")

# Prepare phylum-level data for the table
table_data_phylum <- per_taxon_summary_phylum %>%
  dplyr::filter(phylum %in% phyla_of_interest) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(is_phylum = TRUE)

# Prepare genus-level data for the table
table_data_genus <- per_taxon_summary_genus %>%
  dplyr::filter(genus %in% genera_of_interest) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(genus_phylum_map, by = "genus") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(is_phylum = FALSE)

# Combine and structure the data for the hierarchical table
hierarchical_table_data <- dplyr::bind_rows(table_data_phylum, table_data_genus) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(Phylum_Group = coalesce(phylum, genus)) %>%
  
  dplyr::mutate(
    # Create the "mean ± sd" display strings
    illumina_display = paste0(round(mean_illumina, 1), " ± ", round(sd_illumina, 1)),
    nanopore_display = paste0(round(mean_nanopore, 1), " ± ", round(sd_nanopore, 1))
  ) %>%  
  
  # Create the Taxon display string using Markdown for styling
  dplyr::mutate(Taxon = if_else(
    is_phylum,
    # For phylum rows, make the name bold
    paste0("***", phylum, "***"),
    # For genus rows, keep the prefix normal and make the name italic
    paste0("└─ *", genus, "*")
  )) %>%
  
  #dplyr::arrange(Phylum_Group, desc(is_phylum), Taxon) %>%
  
  # Arrange by the group, then put the phylum row at the top,
  # then arrange the genera by their mean abundance in Illumina (descending)
  dplyr::arrange(Phylum_Group, desc(is_phylum), desc(mean_illumina)) %>%
  
  
  # Select only the final columns needed for the table
  dplyr::select(Phylum_Group, Taxon, is_phylum, illumina_display, nanopore_display)
  
# --- 2. Create the styled gt table ---
hierarchical_bias_table_gt <- hierarchical_table_data %>%
  gt() %>%
  # Hide the helper column from the final output
  cols_hide(columns = c(Phylum_Group, is_phylum)) %>%
  
  # Tell gt to render the Markdown formatting in the 'Taxon' column
  fmt_markdown(columns = Taxon) %>% 
  
  # Style the phylum rows (the parent rows) to be bold
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = is_phylum == TRUE
    )
  ) %>%
  
  # Set table title
  tab_header(
    title = "Table 3 Examples of taxa with strong platform-specific bias"
  ) %>%
  
  # Format the numeric mean columns
  fmt_number(
    columns = where(is.numeric),
    decimals = 2
  ) %>%
  
  # Now, apply a custom transformation to add the prefix 
  text_transform(
  # Target location: numeric columns, but ONLY in rows where it's NOT a phylum
  locations = cells_body(
    columns = c(illumina_display, nanopore_display),
    rows = is_phylum == FALSE
  ),
  # The function to apply:
  fn = function(x) {
    # 'x' is the already formatted number (e.g., "5.82")
    # Prepend the symbol to it
    paste("└─", x)
  }
) %>%
  
  # Set column labels and spanner
  cols_label(
    Taxon = "Taxon",
    illumina_display = "Illumina",
    nanopore_display = "ONT"
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(label = "Mean Abundance ± SD (%)",
              columns = c(illumina_display, nanopore_display)) %>%
  
  # Set alignment (Taxon left, others center)
  cols_align(align = "left", columns = Taxon) %>%
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = where(is.numeric)) %>%
  
  # Set modern table options
  tab_options(
    column_labels.font.weight = "bold",
    table_body.hlines.style = "none",
    column_labels.border.bottom.style = "solid",
    column_labels.border.bottom.width = px(2),
    table.border.top.style = "none",
    table.border.bottom.style = "none"
  ) %>%
  
  # Set source note
  tab_source_note(source_note = "Values represent the mean relative abundance (%) across four samples.")

# Display and save the table
gtsave(hierarchical_bias_table_gt, filename = "tables/Table3_hierarchical_bias_table.png")
gtsave(hierarchical_bias_table_gt, filename = "tables/Table3_hierarchical_bias_table.rtf" )

hierarchical_bias_table_gt
```
```{r}
# --- 3.5 Identifying Exclusively Detected Genera ---

# 1. Genera detected ONLY by Illumina (mean ONT abundance is 0)
illumina_exclusive_genera <- per_taxon_summary_genus %>%
  dplyr::filter(mean_illumina > 0 & mean_nanopore == 0) %>%
  # Sort by the most abundant ones first
  dplyr::arrange(desc(mean_illumina))


# 2. Genera detected ONLY by ONT (mean Illumina abundance is 0)
nanopore_exclusive_genera <- per_taxon_summary_genus %>%
  dplyr::filter(mean_nanopore > 0 & mean_illumina == 0) %>%
  # Sort by the most abundant ones first
  dplyr::arrange(desc(mean_nanopore))


# --- 3. Print the results to fill in your manuscript ---

# Number of genera found only in Illumina
num_illumina_exclusive <- nrow(illumina_exclusive_genera)
# Get the name of the most abundant one as an example
example_illumina_exclusive <- illumina_exclusive_genera$genus[1]

print(paste("Illumina-exclusive genera count:", num_illumina_exclusive))
print(paste("Most abundant Illumina-exclusive genus:", example_illumina_exclusive))


# Number of genera found only in ONT
num_nanopore_exclusive <- nrow(nanopore_exclusive_genera)
# Get the name of the most abundant one as an example
example_nanopore_exclusive <- nanopore_exclusive_genera$genus[1]

print(paste("ONT-exclusive genera count:", num_nanopore_exclusive))
print(paste("Most abundant ONT-exclusive genus:", example_nanopore_exclusive))
```

```{r}
# --- 3.6 Save Supplementary Tables as a Single Excel File ---

# Combine all bias summary tables into a list
# Each element name will become an Excel sheet name
bias_summary_sheets <- list(
  "Phylum" = per_taxon_summary_phylum,
  "Family" = per_taxon_summary_family,
  "Genus"  = per_taxon_summary_genus,
  "Species" = per_taxon_summary_species
)

# Define the output filename
xlsx_filename <- "tables/Supplementary_Table_S2.xlsx"

# Write to Excel file
writexl::write_xlsx(bias_summary_sheets, path = xlsx_filename)

print(paste("Saved bias summary tables to:", xlsx_filename))
```
